full_response:
Let  

  X_t ∈ {0,1,2,… ,n} = length of the line at the beginning of minute t.  

While 1 ≤ X_t ≤ n–1 the evolution in one minute is

 • Barr serves one lemonade  → line length decreases by 1,  
 • with probability p a pair of friends arrives  → length increases by 2,  
 • with probability 1–p nobody arrives.

Hence, for 1 ≤ i ≤ n–1 we have the one–step transition probabilities  

  P(i,i+1)=p    (because –1 for the service and +2 for the arrival)  
  P(i,i–1)=1–p.  

(States 0 and n have slightly different outgoing probabilities, but the above two
probabilities are the only ones that connect i with i±1, 0≤i≤n–1.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Showing π_{i}=((p)/(1-p))^{i}π_{0} for 0≤i≤n–1
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Because the chain is irreducible and aperiodic on the finite set {0,…,n}, it
possesses a unique stationary distribution π⃗ satisfying π⃗P = π⃗.

Key observation (detailed balance on the interior edges).  
In stationarity, the expected number of transitions
from any state i to i+1 must equal the expected number of transitions from
i+1 back to i:

  π_i P(i,i+1) = π_{i+1} P(i+1,i).   (∗)

For 0 ≤ i ≤ n–1, P(i,i+1)=p and P(i+1,i)=1–p, so (∗) gives

  π_i·p = π_{i+1}·(1–p)  
   ⇒ π_{i+1} = (p/(1–p)) π_i.                     (1)

Relation (1) expresses π_{i+1} as a constant multiple of π_i, therefore by
repeated substitution:

  π_1 = (p/(1–p))π_0  
  π_2 = (p/(1–p))π_1 = (p/(1–p))^2 π_0  
     ⋯  
  π_i = (p/(1–p))^i π_0 for every 0≤i≤n–1.

This is exactly the required formula.

(π_0 will later be fixed by the normalisation
∑_{i=0}^{n}π_i = 1; note that the above relation need not hold for the last
state i = n because its transition probabilities differ.)

\boxed{\displaystyle \pi_i=\left(\frac{p}{\,1-p\,}\right)^{i}\pi_0\quad\text{for}\;0\le i\le n-1}

Confidence: 77%
